plant_profile.history: Unique History: Shyonaka (also known as Sonapatha or the 'Broken Bones Tree' due to the appearance of its large dangling pods) is one of the most important Dashamoola (ten roots) herbs in classical Ayurveda. It is grouped under Laghu Panchamoola (the five smaller/lighter roots). Its usage is documented extensively in Charaka Samhita, Sushruta Samhita, and Ashtanga Hridayam for digestive, respiratory, and inflammatory disorders. Physical Characteristics: A small to medium-sized deciduous tree, typically growing to 10-15 meters, with very few branches, giving it a distinctive appearance. The bark is soft, spongy, and light grey. The leaves are very large (can be 1-2 meters long), bi- or tri-pinnately compound, clustered at the top of branches. Flowers are large, fleshy, reddish-purple to brownish, and bloom nocturnally (bat-pollinated), appearing in large erect racemes. The fruit is a very long, flat, sword-like capsule (30-60 cm long), containing many winged seeds. Found in sub-Himalayan tracts, northeastern India, and various deciduous forests. Specific Chemical Compounds: Major bioactive constituents include flavonoids (baicalein, chrysin, oroxylin A, scutellarein—these are potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant flavones rarely found in this concentration in other plants), isoflavones (biochanin A), glycosides (tetuin), tannins (ellagic acid), phytosterols (β-sitosterol), and alkaloids. Baicalein is a primary marker compound and is responsible for a significant portion of the plant's documented anti-inflammatory and anti-diarrheal activity. Strict Standardizations: As per API, the root bark and stem bark of Oroxylum indicum are standardized. Key quality tests include foreign matter, total ash, acid-insoluble ash, alcohol-soluble extractive, and water-soluble extractive values. HPTLC fingerprinting using marker compound baicalein is recommended for authentication and quality assurance, aligning with WHO guidelines for quality control of herbal materials.

ayurvedic_properties.dosha_modulation: Primarily pacifies Vata and Kapha doshas. Its Tikta (bitter) Rasa, Laghu (light) and Ruksha (dry) Gunas, and Ushna (hot) Virya work to reduce Kapha accumulation and pacify deranged Vata, especially in the respiratory and digestive tracts.

medicinal_uses.anti_diarrheal_and_dysentery__grahi_: Shyonaka is one of the most potent Grahi (absorbent/anti-diarrheal) herbs in Ayurveda. Its strong astringent (Kashaya) and binding (Stambhana) properties make it highly effective in managing acute and chronic diarrhea (Atisara) and dysentery (Pravahika), by absorbing excess fluid in the intestines and reducing gut motility.
medicinal_uses.anti_inflammatory_and_analgesic__shothag: As a key Dashamoola ingredient, it is extensively used for inflammatory conditions, especially those of Vata origin, such as rheumatoid arthritis (Amavata), joint pain, and neuralgia. Its anti-inflammatory activity has been validated in pharmacological studies.
medicinal_uses.respiratory_disorders: Effective against cough (Kasa), asthma (Shwasa), and bronchitis, particularly when caused by aggravated Kapha. Its Ushna Virya helps liquefy and expel thick, tenacious mucus from the respiratory passages.
medicinal_uses.digestive_stimulant__deepana_pachana_: Enhances the digestive fire (Agni) and aids in the digestion and metabolism of Ama (toxins). This makes it useful for anorexia (Aruchi) and dyspepsia (Ajirna).
medicinal_uses.antipyretic__jwarahara_: Used traditionally in the management of various types of fevers (Jwara), particularly those associated with Vata and Kapha vitiation.
medicinal_uses.chemical_constituents: Contains significant bioactive compounds including baicalein (a potent flavone with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity), chrysin (another flavone), oroxylin A, scutellarein, tetuin (a glycoside), biochanin A (an isoflavone), ellagic acid, and β-sitosterol. Baicalein is considered a key marker compound for this species.

preparation_methods.decoction__kwatha_: Take 10-15g of dried Shyonaka root bark, coarsely crush it, and boil in 200ml of water until reduced to approximately 50ml (1/4th). Strain and consume warm, twice daily, for diarrhea, dysentery, and digestive complaints.
preparation_methods.powder__churna_: The stem bark or root bark is shade-dried, finely powdered, and sieved (80-100 mesh). Consumed with warm water, buttermilk, or honey. Effective for digestive stimulation and mild anti-inflammatory action.
preparation_methods.dashamoolarishtam: A classical fermented Ayurvedic preparation where Shyonaka root bark is one of ten key root ingredients. The roots are processed according to AFI guidelines for Arishta/Asava preparation, involving decoction, addition of jaggery and fermenting agents, and fermentation for a specified period.
preparation_methods.topical_paste__lepa_: Fresh or dried bark is ground with water to form a paste, applied externally over inflamed or painful joints and areas of localized swelling.
preparation_methods.medicated_oil: The bark decoction or paste is processed with sesame oil (Tila Taila) for external application in Vata disorders, joint pain, and muscular stiffness.

dosage.adults: Powder (Churna): 3-6 grams per day, in divided doses, with warm water or buttermilk. Decoction (Kwatha): 50-100 ml per day, in divided doses. Dashamoolarishtam: 15-30 ml with an equal quantity of water, twice daily, after meals.
dosage.children: Not typically indicated for young children unless specifically prescribed for diarrhea or dysentery by a qualified Ayurvedic pediatrician. Doses, if used, are typically 1/4 to 1/2 of the adult dose, usually as a diluted decoction.
dosage.elderly: Standard adult dosages are generally applicable. Monitor for excessive drying effects due to its Ruksha (dry) Guna, especially in individuals with Vata-predominant constitutions. Moderate hydration should be maintained.

contraindications: Due to its strong Grahi (absorbent/binding) and Stambhana (stopping) properties, Shyonaka is strictly contraindicated in individuals with chronic constipation (Vibandha) or severe Vata-type dryness of the intestines. Its use requires careful differentiation of the type of diarrhea/dysentery, as using it when Ama (toxins) is still present in the gut can lead to complications.
side_effects: Generally safe within recommended doses. Excessive doses or prolonged use without medical supervision may cause severe constipation, abdominal discomfort, or excessive dryness of the mucous membranes due to its potent astringent and drying qualities.
drug_interactions: Caution is advised when co-administered with modern anti-diarrheal drugs (e.g., loperamide) due to potential additive constipating effects. May theoretically interact with anti-diabetic drugs, as some studies show mild hypoglycemic potential. Flavonoids like baicalein can interact with CYP450 enzymes, potentially affecting the metabolism of certain allopathic drugs.
safety.toxicity_notes: GRAS Status: Shyonaka has a long history of safe use in traditional Ayurvedic medicine as part of the Dashamoola group. It is listed in the API and recognized by the Ministry of AYUSH. Toxicological studies on Oroxylum indicum extracts generally indicate a safe profile at therapeutic doses.
